coredata.dc:title: A simple one-step synthesis of melanin-originated red shift emissive carbonaceous dots for bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Chuan
Liu, Yongmei
Chen, Jiantao
He, Qin
Gao, Huile
coredata.dc:description: Carbonaceous dots (CDs) are superior nanomaterials owing to their promising luminescence properties and good biocompatibility. However, most CDs have relatively short excitation/emission, which restrict their application in bioimaging. In this study, a simple one-step procedure was developed for synthesis of melanin-originated CDs (MNPs). The MNPs showed two long red shift emissions at 570nm and 645nm with broad absorptions from 200nm to 400nm and 500nm to 700nm, suggesting the great potential of MNPs in bioimaging. Besides, several experiments indicated that MNPs possessed good serum stability and well blood compatibility. In vitro, MNPs could be taken up by C6 cell in a concentration- and time-dependent manner with endosomes involved. In conclusion, MNPs were prepared using a simple one-step method with unique optical and good biological properties and could be used for bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonaceous dots
Red shift emission
Bioimaging
Melanin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of MNPs. (A) TEM image of MNPs, bar represents 50nm. (B) FT-IR spectrum of MNPs. (C) XPS survey spectrum of MNPs. (D) XPS C1S spectrum of MNPs. (E) XPS N1s spectrum of MNPs. (F) XPS O1s spectrum of MNPs. (G) The absorbance spectra of water-dispersible MNPs. The inset showed enlarged absorbance spectra of the MNPs from 600 to 800nm. (H) Fluorescent spectrum of MNPs. The inner pictures showed MNPs in bright light (a) and UV light (b) respectively.

(A) Stability of MNP in different concentration of FBS. (B, C) Hemolysis rate (B) and image (C) of 2% red blood cells after incubation with different concentration of MNPs for 8h. (D) Cytotoxicity of MNPs to bEnd. 3 cells after 24h incubation. (n=6).

(A) Fluorescence intensity of C6 cells with 125μg/mL for different times (n=3). (B) Fluorescence intensity of C6 cells at different concentration for 1h (n=3). (C) C6 cell uptake of MNP at different concentration for 1h, bar represents 20μm.
Subcellular localization of MNP with endosome after 0.25h or 4h incubation, bar represents 20μm.

head.ce:title: A simple one-step synthesis of melanin-originated red shift emissive carbonaceous dots for bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Drug Targeting and Drug Delivery Systems, West China School of Pharmacy, Sichuan University, No. 17, Block 3, Southern Renmin Road, Chengdu 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Drug Targeting and Drug Delivery Systems

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Thoracic Oncology, Cancer Center and State Key Laboratory of Biotherapy, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cancer Center and State Key Laboratory of Biotherapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbonaceous dots (CDs) are superior nanomaterials owing to their promising luminescence properties and good biocompatibility. However, most CDs have relatively short excitation/emission, which restrict their application in bioimaging. In this study, a simple one-step procedure was developed for synthesis of melanin-originated CDs (MNPs). The MNPs showed two long red shift emissions at 570nm and 645nm with broad absorptions from 200nm to 400nm and 500nm to 700nm, suggesting the great potential of MNPs in bioimaging. Besides, several experiments indicated that MNPs possessed good serum stability and well blood compatibility. In vitro, MNPs could be taken up by C6 cell in a concentration- and time-dependent manner with endosomes involved. In conclusion, MNPs were prepared using a simple one-step method with unique optical and good biological properties and could be used for bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnologies have been developed for more than 40years to deliver therapeutic and diagnostic agents in a safe and more efficient manner [1,2]. Until now, several nanomedicines and nanoprobes have been approved for clinical use. However, the properties of these nanomedicines and nanoprobes are far from fully addressing clinical requirements. Fluorescent carbonaceous dots (CDs) are new kinds of nanomaterials that emerged several years ago [3–7]. CDs has been used in sensoring [8], tumor imaging [9], drug delivery [10] and photothermal therapy [11] because of their intrinsic advantages, including good biocompatibility, high fluorescent intensity, good photo stability, excellent water solubility, simple synthesis and modification [12]. Currently, many precursors are used for preparation of CDs, such as silk, graphene, saccharide, amino acid, polymer, and many other carbon contained materials [13–15]. However, the luminescence of most CDs are still with short excitation and emission wavelengths, which is not suitable for in vivo imaging because of the poor tissue penetration and high background. Therefore, preparing CDs with red shift luminescence is still a challenge for researchers.

Recently, we prepared CDs from glycine using simple heat treatment method, and the luminescence successfully extended to 500–600nm [15]. Although it could be used for in vivo glioma imaging, the fluorescence intensity at 600nm was much weaker than that at 500nm. To further improve the luminescence efficiency with a red-shift wavelength, we developed a one-step method to prepare CDs using melanin as the only precursor. Melanin was a common material in our body, as well as an effective absorber of light [16–18]. Directly hydrothermal method of melanin for 48h could obtain the melanin originated CDs (MNPs) with a size of 45nm. Then the luminescence properties of MNPs were carefully evaluated. The particle size, surface groups, serum stability, hemcompatibility and cytotoxicity were performed to characterize the MNPs. Moreover, C6 cell imaging and endosome labeling were also carried out to probe the bioimaging potential of MNPs.

Melanin (99%) was purchased from Keddia Reagent (Chengdu, China). DAPI and thiazolyl blue (MTT) were purchased from Beyotime (Haimen, China). LysoTracker Red DND-99 was purchased from Life technologies (Grand Island, NY, USA), Plastic cell culture dishes and plates were obtained from Wuxi NEST Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Wuxi, China). Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium cell culture medium (DMEM) and FBS were obtained from Life Technologies (Grand Island, NY, USA). bEnd.3 Cell line was obtained from the Institute of Biochemistry and Cell Biology, Shanghai Institutes for Biological Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). All of the other chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade.

MNPs were obtained from directly hydrothermal method of about 1.0g of water-soluble melanin and 6mL deionized water at 220°C for 48h. After cooled to room temperature, the solution was filtered (pore size 0.45μm). Morphology of MNPs was captured by transmission electronic microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI, USA). Zeta potential was determined by a Malvern Zetasizer (Malvern, NanoZS, UK). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra in water was recorded using a Varian cary 100 conc UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). Fluorecence spectroscopy was recorded using a Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra in KBr was collected on a Bruker Vector 22 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an AXIS Ultra DLD (Kratos, UK) with Mg Ka radiation (hν =1486.6eV), with a chamber pressure of 2.2×10−9 Torr. Fluorescence quantum yield was measured against Quinine in 0.1N sulfuric acid (literature QY 0.546 at 365nm) as a standard using RF-5301PC spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

The stability of MNP was investigated in PBS with different concentrations of FBS, MNPs were suspended in 0%, 10% or 50% FBS and incubated in a shaker (37°C, 75rpm). The absorption of MNPs at 490nm was detected by a microplate reader (Multiskan MK3, Thermo, USA) at 0, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 and 24h. Zeta potential was determined by a Malvern Zetasizer (Malvern, NanoZS, UK) at 0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12 and 24h.

Whole blood was collected from BALB/c mice using heparin as the anticoagulant. After centrifugation at 2000rpm for 5min, the red blood cells were resuspended in PBS (pH 7.4) to get 2% erythrocyte stock dispersion (ESD). Different concentrations of MNPs were added in 2% ESD and incubated at 37°C for different time. The absorption at 490nm was detected by a microplate reader (Thermo Scientific Varioskan Flash, USA). 1% of Triton X-100 was used as a positive control while PBS (PH7.4) was used as negative control.

The cytotoxicity of MNPs was evaluated by MTT assay. bEnd. 3 cells (5×103 per well) were seeded in 96-well plates and allowed to grow until 60% confluent. MNPs were added into the wells at a series of concentrations ranging from 128μg/mL to 1μg/mL for another 24h incubation, then 10μL MTT solution (5mg/mL) was added into each well and incubated for 4h. After the medium was replaced by 150μL dimethyl sulfoxide, the absorbance was measured by a microplate reader (Thermo Scientific Varioskan Flash, USA) at 490nm.

For quantitative studies, C6 cells were seeded in 24-well plates at a density of 5×105 cells per well and incubated for 24h. 500μg/mL, 125μg/mL and 31μg/mL of MNPs were added into the wells. After incubation for 0.15h, 1h, 2h and 4h at 37°C, the cells were washed three times with PBS, trypsinized, and resuspended in 0.5mL PBS (pH 7.4). The fluorescent intensity of cells was analyzed by a flow cytometer (Cytomics FC 500, Beckman Coulter, USA).

For qualitative measurement, C6 cells were seeded onto 12mm coverslips in 6-well plate at a density of 1×105 cells per well and incubated at 37°C for 24h. Then the cells were treated with different concentrations of MNPs for different time. After incubation, the cells were washed, fixed and stained with 0.5μg/mL DAPI. then Imaged by a confocal microscope (LSM710, Carl Zeiss Germany).

C6 cells were seeded in plates as described in qualitative measurement of cellular uptake and incubated with 125μg/mL for 4h and 0.25h. At 0.5h before the incubation ended, LysoTracker Red DND-99 (100nM) was added. After incubation, the cells were washed, fixed and stained with 0.5mg/mL of DAPI. Then the cells were imaged by a confocal microscope (LSM710, Carl Zeiss, Germany).

The synthetic approaches of CDs have been extensively developed, including top-down and bottom-up methods. The top-down methods are mainly based on the chemical or physical cutting processes of relatively macroscopic carbon structures [19]. For the bottom-up methods, organic precursors are required as seeds to grow into CDs under certain conditions [12]. The hydrothermal method belongs to the bottom-up methods, which is widely used because it is a simple and controllable procedure. In this study, directly hydrothermal method of melanin at 220°C for 48h could obtain MNPs as a deep blue dispersion. MNPs were about 45nm with a uniform dispersion which was confirmed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 1 A). Due to low contrast of CDs, the TEM picture of MNPs was not as clear as other kinds of nanoparticles, which was similar to our previous studies [15,20]. Zeta potential of MNPs was 6.2mV in de-ionized water. However, the zeta potential was different in PBS with various pH values (Fig. S1). At pH 7.4, the zeta potential turned to −19.3mV, which may be due to the release of H+ from surface groups of MNPs in high pH value. To further characterize the MNPs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis were performed. In the FTIR analysis of MNPs, the following vibrations were observed (Fig. 1B): stretching vibration of COH at 3418.20cm−1, stretching vibration of NH at 3118.66cm−1, asymmetric stretching vibration of CNHC at 1172.09cm−1, stretching vibration of CC at 1599.82cm−1, 1495.15cm−1 and 1401cm−1, and stretching vibration of CH at 3012.61cm−1, which belonged to the aromatic structure of the benzene ring. The FTIR study indicated that MNPs contained many polycyclic aromatic and CN groups, which was consistent with our previous studies [21,22]. These groups are essential for fluorescence and further surface modification.

The XPS spectrum displayed three peaks at 284.9, 400.9 and 532.5eV (Fig. 1C), which were attributed to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1D) showed four peaks at 284.2, 284.8, 286.0 and 286.6eV, which were contributed by CC, CN, CO and CN/CO groups, respectively [23]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) showed three peaks at 401.3, 401.6 and 402.4eV, owing to CNC, NC and NH bands, respectively [24]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) displayed two peaks at 531.5 and 532.4eV, indicating the CO and COH/COC groups, respectively [8]. These result further corroborated that the surface of MNPs was rich in oxygen and nitrogen function groups, which were useful for further modification.

The UV–vis spectrum of MNPs showed two ranges of broad absorptions from 200nm to 400nm and 500nm to 700nm (Fig. 1G). Correspondingly, MNPs had a wide excitation range from 440nm to 615nm with two maximum emissions at 570nm and 645nm (Fig. 1H), which was considerably longer than the emission of previous published CDs [15,21]. The longer emission of MNPs enables them suitable for in vivo bioimaging because of the deeper penetration and lower background. When increased excitation wavelength from 440nm to 520nm, the emission at 570nm gradually elevated, and during 500nm to 615nm, the emission at 645nm also elevated according to the increase of excitation wavelength, suggesting longer wavelength was more efficient in exciting the fluorescence of MNPs. This property was important for in vivo imaging due to the deeper penetration of longer wavelength of light. The fluorescence quantum yield of the MNPs was 18.0% using Quinine in 0.1N sulfuric acid (literature QY 0.546 at 365nm) as a standard. These results demonstrated MNPs had excellent photoluminescence properties and enabled MNPs with greatly potential in bioimaging.

Serum stability was evaluated through incubation MNPs with different concentrations of fetal bovine serum (FBS) for different time. The MNPs absorption at 490nm was increased gradually, which was positively related to the incubation time and the concentration of serum (Fig. 2 A). At 12h, the absorbance of MNPs with FBS increased greatly, suggesting longer incubation time could lead to higher protein adsorption and the protein adsorption may cause aggregation. Zeta potential of those groups changed fast in the first 4h. The zeta potential of MNPs with different concentrations of FBS was consistent with the blank FBS group (Fig. S2), suggesting that MNPs could interact with serum proteins and resulted in protein adsorption on the surface of MNPs. Owing to the extremely high surface to volume ratio of particles, protein adsorption become a very common phenomenon when particles come in contact with serum or protein [25]. MNPs have active surface chemistry in comparison to bulk biomaterials, hence, they tend to reduce their large surface energy by interaction with the serum proteins in which they are dispersed [26], which would change the in vivo behaviors of nanoparticles, and needed to be further evaluated.

Furthermore, hemocompatibility was evaluated through incubation MNPs with 2% ESD. Despite increasing the concentration of MNPs promoted the hemolysis rate, the hemolysis rates at different concentrations of MNPs were all lower than 8% after 8h incubation (Fig. 2B), which was much lower than our previously reported fluorescent carbon nanospheres prepared from spider silk [21]. This result suggested MNPs had low hemolysis potential, which was suitable for intravenous application.

For in vivo application, the toxicity of MNPs to cells was another issue should be considered. MTT assay was carried out on bEnd. 3 cells. The cell viability presented no obvious difference between control and MNPs treated cells for 24h at concentration as high as 64μg/mL (Fig. 2D), suggesting MNPs have relatively low cytotoxicity. Further elevated the concentration of MNPs to 128μg/mL could considerably inhibit the cell growth. The toxicity of MNPs was higher than CDs which we have studied in recent years, may due to the zeta potential was positive in low pH value, resulted in more MNPs accumulated in the nuclei. However, the toxicity could be attenuated after surface modification, such as PEGylation [22], which needs further evaluation.

To evaluate the cell imaging potential of MNPs, C6 glioma cells were used for cellular uptake study. As determined by flow cytometry, increased the incubation time from 0.25h to 4h led to enhance the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3 A), suggesting that the uptake was time-dependent. Additionally, increased the concentration of MNPs from 31μg/mL to 500μg/mL also elevated the cellular uptake intensity (Fig. 3B), suggesting that the cellular uptake was concentration-dependent, which was consistent with most of other particles [27–29]. Then, we used confocal laser scanning microscope to further investigate the C6 glioma cell imaging potential. It was shown that MNPs could be effectively taken up by C6 cells and resulted in bright fluorescence (Fig. 3C). These studies suggested MNPs could be used for cell imaging.

To further evaluate the intracellular behavior of MNPs, colocalization of MNPs with endosomes was evaluated through fluorescent labeling. After 0.25h incubation, some of MNPs colocalized with lysoTracker labeled endosomes, while some of MNPs distributed in the whole cells. After 4h incubation, most MNPs distributed in the cytoplasm rather than endosomes (Fig. 4 ). These results suggested the uptake of MNPs may mediate by endosomes and MNPs could escape from endosomes quickly. Additionally, it was also obvious that MNPs could enter into nuclei, which may contribute to the relative high cytotoxicity than previous CDs [15,21].

In this study, we prepared a new melanin-originated CD through a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The MNPs showed two long red shift emission wavelengths with relative high quantum yield, making it a potential probe for bioimaging. Low hemolysis rate and well serum stability of MNPs contributed great potential for further intravenous application. In vitro, MNPs could be taken up by C6 cell in a concentration- and time-dependent manner. C6 cell imaging and endosome labeling demonstrated the great potential of MNPs in bioimaging. However, MNPs could considerably inhibit cell growth at 128μg/mL, which need further optimization before in vivo application. Besides, in-depth knowledge about the in vivo behavior of MNPs is warranted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was granted by National Natural Science Foundation of China (31571016, 81402866).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2016.07.007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Eur. J. Pharm. Biopharm. Off. J. Arbeitsgemeinschaft Für Pharmazeutische Verfahrenstechnik E V
